sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Amanda Taylor, is a 72-year-old woman who comes for follow-up for hypertension.
She has a history of significant alcohol use disorder and mild hypercholesterolemia.
At her last visit, her blood pressure was 130/90.
She reports struggling with not drinking and mentions that she has been drinking 4-5 times a week, consuming hard alcohol.
She last had a drink on Monday and has gone a couple of days without drinking since then.
She takes hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg by mouth once daily and lisinopril 10 mg by mouth once daily, but has been taking only half the dose of lisinopril due to side effects.
Past medical history: Significant alcohol use disorder, mild hypercholesterolemia.
Review of systems: The patient denies chest tightness or pressure, shortness of breath, fever, cough, or sore throat.
Current medications: Hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg by mouth once daily; Lisinopril 10 mg by mouth once daily (note: patient is taking only half the prescribed dose).
Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient is alert, pleasant, in no acute distress.
Her skin is moist.
No tremor, cva tenderness, or murmur is present.
Chest is clear to percussion and auscultation, with unlabored breathing.
Cardiac rhythm is regular.
No gallop is present.
JVP is flat with a head at 90 degrees.
The patient does not appear anxious, agitated, or depressed.
Test Results: Not mentioned.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has an alcohol use disorder and hypertension.
Problem list: Alcohol use disorder; Hypertension; Mild hypercholesterolemia; Unknown.
Plan:
I plan to start the patient on naltrexone injections, which will be given about once a month.
I will see her back in 2 months to assess her progress.
I recommend upgrading her influenza vaccination and Shingrix #2, which she previously deferred.
Follow-up:
The patient will return for her first naltrexone injection on May 17.
I will see her again in June to assess her progress after the second injection.
